quick_facts.label: Core qualification
quick_facts.value: The standard qualifying degree is Ayurvedacharya (Bachelor of Ayurvedic Medicine and Surgery, BAMS). Under NCISM’s undergraduate Ayurveda regulations, the programme is five years and six months: three 18-month professional phases plus a 12-month compulsory rotatory internship.

quick_facts.label: Practice and registration
quick_facts.value: A practitioner is a registered Ayurveda, Unani, Siddha or Sowa-Rigpa medical practitioner entered in a State Register or the National Register. For AYUSH students admitted from academic year 2021–22 onward, NCISM identifies the National Exit Test (NExT) as applicable; its examination regulations provide that qualifying NExT and completing the internship are required for registration and for clinical practice where registration is mandatory.

quick_facts.label: Clinical remit
quick_facts.value: BAMS education covers Ashtanga Ayurveda and practical clinical training; the regulated curriculum includes general/internal medicine, surgery, eye/ENT/oro-dentistry, obstetrics and paediatrics, toxicology, mental-health-related subjects, preventive/rejuvenative care and reproductive medicine.

quick_facts.label: Private-practice obligations
quick_facts.value: A registered practitioner must display the registration certificate in the clinic and put the registration number on prescriptions, certificates and receipts. The ethics regulations also require patient-record retention or handover after OPD consultation, confidentiality safeguards, and appropriate referral where the case is outside the practitioner’s training, experience or available facilities.

quick_facts.label: Continuing professional development
quick_facts.value: The ethics regulations make continuing medical education relevant to registration renewal: practitioners must secure at least 50 CME credit points in the five years before each renewal.

sections.heading: What a Private Ayurvedic Practitioner does
paragraphs: A private Ayurvedic practitioner provides patient-facing clinical care within the education, training and registration held. The BAMS curriculum is designed to produce graduates with knowledge of Ashtanga Ayurveda, contemporary advances and extensive practical training for health-care services; its clinical training spans OPD, IPD and specialty settings including Kayachikitsa, Panchakarma, Shalya Tantra, Shalakya Tantra, women’s health and paediatrics.

In independent or visiting practice, professional work extends beyond consultation and treatment planning. NCISM’s ethics rules require maintaining or handing over patient records, protecting confidentiality, displaying registration credentials, using registration particulars on prescriptions and receipts, counselling patients on lifestyle where relevant, treating emergencies to the best of the practitioner’s ability and available facilities, and referring cases when appropriate.
bullets: Assess patients and formulate Ayurveda-based care within the practitioner’s recognised qualification, training and available facilities.
Document consultations and maintain secure patient records; comply with the rules for certificates, prescriptions and registration disclosure.

Explain care and lifestyle recommendations, arrange consultation or referral for difficult, serious, out-of-scope or inadequately equipped cases, and provide a case summary when referring to a specialist.
Maintain ethical professional conduct, including transparent clinic/waiting-room fee display and avoidance of improper patient solicitation or product endorsement.

sections.heading: Route into private Ayurvedic practice
paragraphs: The usual route begins with admission to a recognised BAMS programme. NCISM’s undergraduate regulations specify a five-and-a-half-year programme, including a compulsory 12-month rotatory internship, culminating in the BAMS degree after passing the required examinations and completing the programme and internship.

Admission to BAMS is made through NEET-UG under the applicable NCISM framework. Current licensure and registration requirements should be checked at the time of graduation: NCISM’s 2026 notice states that NExT applies to AYUSH students admitted from academic year 2021–22 onward, while the National Examination Regulations state that NExT is for licensing and State/National Register enrolment after internship.
bullets: Complete Class 12/equivalent eligibility and qualify NEET-UG as required for the applicable admission cycle.
Complete BAMS academic study and the compulsory rotatory internship at an appropriately recognised training setting.
Qualify NExT where applicable to the student cohort, then obtain enrolment in the relevant State Register or National Register before undertaking registered clinical practice.
Meet state- and establishment-specific requirements that may apply to operating or working in a particular private clinic, hospital or wellness facility.

sections.heading: Skills and professional capabilities
paragraphs: The role requires clinical assessment, diagnostic reasoning, safe treatment planning and communication grounded in BAMS training. NCISM requires clinical exposure from the first professional year onward, including patient assessment activities, specialty OPD/IPD work, prescription familiarity, therapeutic diet exposure and rotations through core clinical departments.

Private practice also requires recordkeeping, privacy protection, referral judgement, emergency responsiveness within training and facilities, and ongoing learning. These are not merely optional business skills: they are addressed in the NCISM ethics and registration framework.
bullets: Ayurvedic clinical assessment and patient counselling, including lifestyle guidance.
Medication, formulation, dosage and therapeutic-procedure knowledge developed through BAMS clinical and internship training.
Clear prescription writing, clinical documentation, confidentiality and registration-compliant professional communication.
Referral and consultation judgement for serious, difficult, out-of-scope or resource-limited cases.
Continuing medical education and updating clinical knowledge and skills.

sections.heading: Where the role is practised
paragraphs: A private Ayurvedic practitioner may work from a clinic/chamber, visit hospitals, or work in private Ayurveda-oriented health-care settings. NCISM’s ethics rules expressly address a practitioner’s clinic, chamber, hospital visits, outpatient records and hospital-related professional duties, which supports these as relevant clinical practice environments.

The setting does not remove professional requirements: the practitioner must practise within recognised qualification and training, maintain records and registration visibility, and make referrals when the required facilities or infrastructure are unavailable.
bullets: Private clinic or consultation chamber.
Private hospital or Ayurveda hospital, including outpatient and inpatient services.
Wellness or specialty settings that deliver clinical services within the practitioner’s registration, competence and facility capabilities.

sections.heading: Progression options
paragraphs: After BAMS, internship and required registration, a practitioner can build experience in private clinical practice and may pursue postgraduate Ayurveda education. NCISM’s National Examination Regulations identify the All India AYUSH Post-Graduate Entrance Test (AIAPGET) as the postgraduate national entrance test; BAMS degree holders who complete internship by the prescribed date are eligible to appear, and registration is required at admission.

Progression can also include deeper clinical focus, leadership in clinics or hospitals, teaching and research. For entry to teaching roles in Indian System of Medicine institutions, NCISM’s 2026 NTET guidance states that eligible applicants require the prescribed Ayurveda qualifications, including BAMS with MD Ayurveda or MS Ayurveda as applicable, and must qualify NTET for first-time appointment as faculty.
bullets: Develop a patient base and clinical expertise in private practice after registration.
Pursue postgraduate Ayurveda education through AIAPGET, subject to the applicable annual eligibility and counselling rules.
Move toward academic roles after the required postgraduate qualification and NTET eligibility, where applicable.
Participate in professional associations, CME, workshops and peer learning to retain registration and develop practice capability.
